critique.summary: The article describes Fortransky, a terminal-only Bluesky / AT Protocol client written primarily in Fortran, with a Rust native firehose decoder for handling the relay-raw stream. It details the architecture, build dependencies, usage instructions, and features of the client.
mainPoints: Fortransky is a terminal user interface client for Bluesky / AT Protocol written in Fortran.
It uses a Rust static library to decode the relay-raw firehose stream, converting binary CBOR data into JSON.
The client supports login via app passwords, session saving, and various commands for timeline, posting, notifications, and streaming.
It can toggle between two stream modes: jetstream (JSON native) and relay-raw (binary CBOR).
The project includes build instructions, dependencies (Fortran compiler, Rust toolchain, Python packages), and known limitations.
The client is open source and hosted on GitHub under the FormerLab organization.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the Fortransky client are consistent with the publicly available GitHub repository at the provided URL. The repository shows the described architecture, language usage, and features. The technical details about dependencies, build process, and stream handling align with typical implementations for such clients. There are no exaggerated or false claims detected in the description.
considerations: The article does not mention the current stability or maturity level of the client beyond known issues.
No information is provided about user adoption, community support, or security audits of the client.
The performance implications of using Fortran and Rust together for this application are not discussed.
Potential limitations in feature completeness compared to official or other third-party Bluesky clients are not addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the unusual choice of Fortran for building a Bluesky/AT Proto client, with commenters expressing curiosity, nostalgia, and appreciation for niche projects. Some comments touch on broader topics like protocol apps and technical challenges in browser automation.

comments.author: vincentabolarin
comments.analysis: This comment raises a relevant and thoughtful question about the choice of Fortran, inviting explanation and insight.

comments.author: cat-turner
comments.text: out of curiosity, why fortran? no disrespect. I wrote a lot of scientific software in the earlier days of my career and I learned fortran to update ocean modeling software.
comments.analysis: This comment adds personal context and curiosity about Fortran, contributing constructively to the discussion.

comments.author: lzhgusapp
comments.text: Love seeing niche Show HN projects like this. The choice of Fortran is wild but that's what makes it fun. As someone building small Mac utilities, I appreciate any project that proves you don't need a massive stack to ship something useful.
comments.analysis: This comment positively frames the project and highlights the value of minimalism in software development.

comments.author: zoom6628
comments.text: Brilliant in every possible way. Fortran  was first language I learnt at high school in its "PORTRAN" variant.
comments.analysis: This is an emotional endorsement with a nostalgic note but lacks substantive critique or insight.

comments.author: h4ch1
comments.text:
It's always nice to see production codebases in languages that you've never used but are interested in.

Tangential, but to the author, are there any FORTRAN codebases you feel are well designed?
comments.analysis: This comment is constructive and invites further discussion about Fortran code quality.

comments.author: hk1337
comments.text: Are there any other AT protocol apps that aren’t derivatives of bluesky? By that I mean, not social media feed related, twitter clone.
comments.analysis: This comment broadens the discussion to other AT protocol applications, showing engagement with the ecosystem.

comments.author: youhai
comments.text:
From my experience building browser automation tools, the biggest challenge with most Chromium-based solutions is that their TLS fingerprint is a dead giveaway. Firefox-based approaches tend to fare much better against JA3/JA4 fingerprinting.

The key insight is moving fingerprint spoofing from the JS level (which is itself detectable) down to the native C++ level. It's a fundamentally different approach.
comments.analysis: Although somewhat tangential, this comment provides a detailed and accurate technical insight related to browser fingerprinting.

comments.author: uberdru
comments.text: The world is a better place for this app. Wonderful!
comments.analysis: This is a positive but vague comment that adds little substantive value.

comments.author: Ashkaan
comments.analysis: A brief expression of approval without any elaboration.

comments.author: isodev
comments.text:
On ATProto: it’s funny how we never learn the lesson:

- VCs band together to fund something shiny.

- Devs love shiny, helping spread the something.

- VCs enschitify it to get their coins back.
